titleStmt.title: SII 1.155: original edition by Eugen Hultzsch
PART IV. ADDENDA. No. 155. AN INSCRIPTION OF KULOTTU·πÑGA-CHO·∏∂A AT CHIDAMBARAM.

summary.p: The subjoined Grantha inscription is engraved on the outside of the east wall of the innermost prƒÅkƒÅra of the great temple at Chidambaram in the South Arcot District. It consists of two verses in the SragdharƒÅ metre, each of which eulogises the victories of Kulottu·πÖga-Cho·∏∑a over the five PƒÅ·πá·∏çyas. The first verse further states, that the king burnt the fort of KorgƒÅra (KorgƒÅra-durga) and defeated the Kera·∏∑as. KorgƒÅra is probably a Sanskritised form of Ko·πükai in the Tinnevelly District, the ancient capital of the PƒÅ·πá·∏çyas.For references on Ko·πükai, see Mr. Sewell's Lists of Antiquities, Vol. I, p. 312. The second verse records, that Kulottu·πÖga-Cho·∏∑a placed a pillar of victory on the SahyƒÅdri mountain, i.e., the Western GhƒÅ·π≠s. This he must have done after his conquest of the Kera·∏∑as, which is mentioned in the first verse.

According to a grant published by Mr. Fleet,Ind. Ant. Vol. XIV, p. 55. Kulottu·πÖga-Cho·∏ça-deva was the name of two of the Eastern Chalukyan successors of the Cho·∏∑a kings. Of the first of these, who was also called RƒÅjendra-Cho·∏ça and ruled from ≈öaka 985 to 1034, the Chell≈´r grant reports that he conquered the Kerala and PƒÅ·πá·∏çya countries.See No. 39, verse 10, p. 59, above. From an unpublished Chidambaram inscriptionSee paragraph 5 of my Progress Report for February, March and April 1888, Madras G.O., 27th July 1888, No. 745, Public. it appears, that the surname Kulottu·πÖga-Cho·∏∑adeva was also borne by the maternal grandfather of the last-mentioned king, the Cho·∏∑a king RƒÅjendra-Cho·∏∑a-deva, among whose conquests we find both the Kera·∏∑a and PƒÅ·πá·∏çya countries.See p. 97, above. Consequently, it is impossible to say to which Kulottu·πÖga-Cho·∏∑a the subjoined inscription has to be referred.This remark applies also to the various kings of the same name, who are mentioned in other inscriptions of this volume. These are:‚ÄîKulottu·πÖga-Cho·∏∑a-deva in No. 86; Ko-RƒÅjakesarivarman, alias Kulottu·πÖga-Cho·∏∑a-deva in Nos. 89, 96 and 130; and Ko·πâeri Me·πâ-ko·πá·∏ça Kulottu·πÖga-Cho·∏∑a-deva in No. 132.

revisionDesc.change: Further conversion of digital encoding to DHARMA encoding scheme according to EGD (Encoding Guide for Diplomatic Editions)

div.p:
svasti ≈õrƒ´ . pƒÅ·πá·∏çyƒÅnda·πá·∏çena jitvƒÅ pracura≈õaramucƒÅ pa√±ca pa√±cƒÅnana≈õrƒ´·∏• dagdhvƒÅ korggƒÅradurggant·πõi·πáamiva sa yathƒÅ
khƒÅ·πá·∏çavam¬∑ pƒÅ·πá·∏çus≈´nu·∏• . pi·π£·π≠vƒÅ tat¬∑ kera·∏∑ƒÅnƒÅm balamatibaha·∏∑am ≈õrƒ´kulottu·πÅgaco·∏∑a≈õcakre ≈õakrapratƒÅpastribhuvanavijayastam¬∑bhamam¬∑bhodhitƒ´re . 1
pu·πáye sa·πÅhyƒÅdri≈õ·πõi·πÅge tribhuvanavijayastam¬∑bhamam¬∑bhodhipƒÅre svacchandam¬∑ pƒÅrasƒ´nƒÅntaru·πáayuvatibhirggƒ´yate yasya kƒ´rtti·∏• .
sa ≈õrƒ´mƒÅnasta≈õatru·∏• prabalabalabharai·∏• pa√±ca pƒÅ·πá·∏çyƒÅnvijitya k·π£ubhyat¬∑ k·π£mƒÅpƒÅlacakram savidhikamakarocchrƒ´kulottu·πÅgaco·∏∑a·∏• . 2

Hail! Prosperity! (Verse 1.) Having defeated the five PƒÅ·πá·∏çyas by an army, which discharged numerous arrows, having burnt, like straw, the fort of KorgƒÅra, just as (Arjuna) the son of PƒÅ·πá·∏çu burnt the KhƒÅ·πá·∏çava (forest),Compare No. 39, verse 3, p. 57, above. and having crushed the extremely dense army of the Kera·∏∑as,‚Äîthe illustrious Kulottu·πÖga-Cho·∏∑a, who resembled Siva in splendour and Indra in might, placed a pillar (commemorative of his) conquest of the three worlds on the shore of the ocean.

(Verse 2.) (Having placedOne would expect a gerund conveying this meaning instead of ambodhipƒÅre, which seems to have crept into the text through the influence of ambodhitƒ´re in verse 1.) a pillar (commemorative of his) conquest of the three worlds on the sacred peak of the SahyƒÅdri (mountain), and having defeated the five PƒÅ·πá·∏çyas by masses of powerful armies,‚Äîthe illustrious Kulottu·πÖga-Cho·∏∑a, whose fame is voluntarily sung by the tender women of the PƒÅrasis,The composer of the inscription mentions the Persians (Tamil pƒÅraci, Sanskrit pƒÅrasƒ´ka) merely as the representatives of the most distant nations which were known to him. and who has driven away his enemies, made the trembling crowd of kings subject to his orders.Savidhika seems to be used in the sense of ƒÅj√±ƒÅvidheya.
Digital edition of SII 1.155 by converted to DHARMA conventions by Emmanuel Francis
